TF-IDF baseline receives the same clinical covariates as the transformer model but different text representations — an unequal comparison that conflates text representation quality with model architecture rather than isolating the contribution of transformer embeddings
all_issues_adjudicated: Primary outcome inconsistency: the experiment is framed as predicting binary 30-day readmission but the primary metric is MAE on a continuous risk score, creating a fundamental objective mismatch that makes results uninterpretable

Preprocessing data leakage: TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the full 36-month dataset including test notes, allowing future clinical terminology distribution to influence training-phase baseline features

Split strategy inconsistency: the design is framed as 'stratified random' but the evaluation sections describe temporal Months 21–26 (validation) and 27–36 (test) boundaries — these are different split paradigms
Clinician sentiment annotation leakage risk: if annotations are made with knowledge of the readmission outcome, the sentiment scalar feature directly encodes label information

TF-IDF baseline receives the same clinical covariates as the transformer model but different text representations — an unequal comparison that conflates text representation quality with model architecture rather than isolating the contribution of transformer embeddings

critic_raw:
This experiment compares a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) against linear regression for ED wait time prediction using 62,000 encounters over 24 months. The temporal split design is well-motivated and correctly implemented (months 1–16 train, 17–20 validation, 21–24 test). The primary metric (MAE in minutes) is clinically interpretable and well-justified. This design is among the stronger in the set, but several issues merit critique.

A preprocessing leakage issue is present: the design states 'all preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting.' The rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is described as 'computed from the full dataset statistics' — this means the rolling statistics for training-period encounters incorporate future test-period arrival patterns in their computation. For a temporal prediction task, this is a form of look-ahead bias. Similarly, StandardScaler parameters derived from the full dataset incorporate test-period encounter statistics.

The TCN input sequence is described as '12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding each check-in,' but the rolling 6-hour mean used as a feature is computed from full-dataset statistics rather than dynamically from available history at decision time. This creates an inconsistency: the TCN is designed to capture real-time temporal patterns, but a key contextual feature (arrival rate) is computed with future information.

The imputation of missing staffing levels and bed counts uses 'median values computed on the entire dataset' — explicitly a full-dataset statistic rather than training-set-only, which is another instance of preprocessing leakage for these operational variables.

The study restricts to encounters where 'patients had previous visits in the last 12 months,' which excludes new patients and walk-in encounters with no prior history. ED wait time prediction in deployment must handle all arrivals; this selection restricts the evaluation to a potentially easier subpopulation and limits generalizability to the full patient flow.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing data leakage: StandardScaler and rolling arrival statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting, incorporating test-period encounter data into training-phase feature normalization
Rolling 6-hour arrival mean feature is computed from full-dataset statistics rather than dynamically from available history, creating look-ahead bias in a real-time operational feature
Imputation of staffing levels and bed counts uses full-dataset medians rather than training-set medians, introducing additional preprocessing leakage for operational variables

Study restricted to patients with prior visits excludes new patients — a potentially easier subpopulation that limits generalizability to full ED patient flow including first-time and walk-in encounters

Paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors may violate independence assumptions if encounters are clustered by day, shift, or staffing configuration — clustered standard errors or mixed-effects modeling would be more appropriate
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing data leakage: StandardScaler and rolling arrival statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting, incorporating test-period encounter data into training-phase feature normalization
Rolling 6-hour arrival mean feature is computed from full-dataset statistics rather than dynamically from available history, creating look-ahead bias in a real-time operational feature
Imputation of staffing levels and bed counts uses full-dataset medians rather than training-set medians, introducing additional preprocessing leakage for operational variables

Study restricted to patients with prior visits excludes new patients — a potentially easier subpopulation that limits generalizability to full ED patient flow including first-time and walk-in encounters

Paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors may violate independence assumptions if encounters are clustered by day, shift, or staffing configuration — clustered standard errors or mixed-effects modeling would be more appropriate
